description: Bordeaux to Sète: 289 miles, 8 days, self-supported cycling

rides.overnightNarrative: Sète, founded in the 17th century to support the Canal du Midi, is a vibrant port city linking inland waterways to the Mediterranean. Known as the “Venice of Languedoc,” its canals define the urban landscape. It hosts the lively water jousting festival, a centuries-old tradition of competitive boat duels. With strong maritime identity, seafood cuisine, and artistic heritage, Sète marks the culmination of the Atlantic-to-Mediterranean journey, blending working port energy with cultural richness.

highlights.title: Bordeaux Historic Waterfront
highlights.description: UNESCO-listed 18th-century port and "Port of the Moon" crescent harbor fueled global commerce.

highlights.title: Roger Lapébie Greenway
highlights.description: Former railway converted to cycling path through vineyards, connecting Bordeaux to rural France.

highlights.title: La Sauve-Majeure Abbey
highlights.description: UNESCO Romanesque ruins, visible landmark on approach to La Réole marking medieval heritage.

routeStops.name: Boul. de la Réole
routeStops.location: Place Georges Chaigne near the center

rides.overnightNarrative: La Réole, one of the oldest towns along the Garonne, traces its roots to a powerful Benedictine priory founded in the 8th century. Its name derives from “Regula,” reflecting monastic rule. The town’s steep streets and arcaded square reveal medieval importance as a trading hub. Each summer, it hosts cultural festivals celebrating music and heritage. Quiet today, La Réole retains a sense of faded authority, with stone facades and river views marking its former strategic and religious significance.
rides.dayShape: Bordeaux → La Réole: 45.7 miles, 1,050 ft elevation. Easy roll out through vineyards. Main climb: Sadirac area (mile 18, 2.3 mi, 240 ft gain). Route passes through Sadirac, Créon, Sauveterre-de-Guyenne. Final push into La Réole.

highlights.title: First sustained Canal de Garonne
highlights.description: Transition into true canal experience with dedicated cycling infrastructure and waterway commerce heritage.

highlights.title: Le Mas-d'Agenais church
highlights.description: Authentic Rembrandt painting in a small village setting, unexpected artistic treasure in countryside.

highlights.title: Aiguillon confluence
highlights.description: Meeting of major waterways with visible lock systems, engineering marvel and water management.

rides.overnightNarrative: Aiguillon sits at the confluence of the Lot and Garonne rivers, a position that made it strategically vital for centuries. The Dukes of Aiguillon once held significant political influence, and the town became associated with regional power struggles. Its fertile surroundings support agriculture, especially plum production for prunes. While modest in scale, Aiguillon reflects classic river-town France, where waterways shaped commerce, defense, and daily life, leaving behind a quietly layered historical identity rooted in geography.
rides.dayShape: La Réole → Aiguillon: 28.4 miles, 120 ft elevation. Flat river valley day following Garonne River. Easy pace with minimal elevation. Route passes through Meilhan-sur-Garonne, Marmande, Le Mas d'Agenais, Clairac, Tonneins, Damazan. Perfect recovery day after Day 1.

highlights.title: Pont-Canal du Cacor
highlights.description: Canal carried across river as major engineering highlight, UNESCO site showcasing 18th-century innovation.

highlights.title: Moissac Abbey
highlights.description: UNESCO Romanesque masterpiece and pilgrimage site along Camino de Santiago route through France.

highlights.title: Abbey cloister and tympanum
highlights.description: Exceptional medieval sculpture detail, among finest Romanesque art in Europe worth studying closely.

rides.overnightNarrative: Moissac is renowned for the Abbey of Saint-Pierre, a UNESCO-listed masterpiece of Romanesque art. Its cloister and sculpted tympanum are considered among the finest in Europe, drawing pilgrims for centuries along the Camino de Santiago. Legend holds that monks once tamed the surrounding land from wilderness through devotion and labor. The town thrives on this spiritual heritage, blending pilgrimage culture with canal-side tranquility, making it a key historical and cultural stop in southwestern France.
rides.dayShape: Aiguillon → Moissac: 47.5 miles, 160 ft elevation. Longer day with gentle rolling terrain transitioning toward Lot River valley. Route passes through Agen, Bon-Encontre, Boé. Moissac marks entry to abbey country with stunning medieval landscape.

highlights.title: Castelsarrasin canal junction
highlights.description: Key inland waterway intersection where multiple canals converge, historic commerce and trade hub.

highlights.title: Canal greenways into Toulouse
highlights.description: Shaded urban riding corridors providing peaceful transition into major city through green infrastructure.

highlights.title: Toulouse "Pink City"
highlights.description: Distinctive terracotta architecture and major regional center, cultural and aerospace industry powerhouse.

routeStops.name: Pillon
routeStops.location: Rue du Rempart Saint-Étienne city center

rides.overnightNarrative: Toulouse, known as “La Ville Rose” for its terracotta brick architecture, has long been a center of culture and innovation. Once a powerful medieval city-state, it later became pivotal in the aerospace industry, home to Airbus headquarters. The Basilica of Saint-Sernin anchors its Romanesque legacy, while lively squares reflect a youthful student population. Toulouse balances deep Occitan heritage with modern dynamism, making it one of France’s most distinctive and economically important regional capitals.
rides.dayShape: Moissac → Toulouse: 44.2 miles, 70 ft elevation. Nearly flat day building toward major city. Steady pace through Castelsarrasin, Valence-d'Agen, Montauban. Final approach into Toulouse—urban cycling as route enters metropolitan area.

highlights.title: Capitole Square & Arcades
highlights.description: Grand civic square framed by pink brick arcades, heart of Toulouse’s daily life.

highlights.title: Basilica of Saint-Sernin
highlights.description: Romanesque pilgrimage church, UNESCO-listed, with octagonal tower and historic Camino significance.

highlights.title: Garonne River Promenade
highlights.description: Tree-lined riverbanks and bridges offer relaxed strolling, sunset views, and classic Pink City perspectives.

rides.overnightNarrative: Toulouse, known as “La Ville Rose” for its terracotta brick architecture, has long been a center of culture and innovation. Once a powerful medieval city-state, it later became pivotal in the aerospace industry, home to Airbus headquarters. The Basilica of Saint-Sernin anchors its Romanesque legacy, while lively squares reflect a youthful student population. Toulouse balances deep Occitan heritage with modern dynamism, making it one of France’s most distinctive and economically important regional capitals.
rides.dayShape: REST DAY — Toulouse. Historic city day for resupply and exploration.

highlights.title: Plane tree canopy
highlights.description: Defining visual of the Canal du Midi, providing shade and character to entire route.

highlights.title: Le Seuil de Naurouze
highlights.description: Canal summit and Atlantic–Mediterranean watershed divide marking pivotal point in journey progression.

highlights.title: Castelnaudary cassoulet birthplace
highlights.description: Canal town birthplace of cassoulet, regional culinary symbol and gastronomic destination for cyclists.

routeStops.name: l'Épi d'Or
routeStops.location: Near the Grand Bassin town center

rides.overnightNarrative: Castelnaudary is widely regarded as the birthplace of cassoulet, the rich bean and meat stew that defines regional cuisine. Legend claims the dish sustained locals during a medieval siege, symbolizing resilience. Positioned along the Canal du Midi, the town became an important waypoint for trade and transport. Its Grand Bassin remains one of the canal’s largest water expanses. Castelnaudary’s identity is deeply tied to food culture, slow travel, and the enduring rhythms of southwestern France.
rides.dayShape: Toulouse → Castelnaudary: 40.1 miles, 190 ft elevation. Gentle rolling exits the city. Transition toward Canal du Midi region. Route climbs slightly through Villefranche-de-Lauragais before arriving at Castelnaudary—the heart of cassoulet country.

highlights.title: Lauragais plains
highlights.description: Wide-open agricultural landscape transition revealing expansive terrain and rural character of region deeply.

highlights.title: Le Somail
highlights.description: One of most picturesque canal villages with authentic charm and character along route.

highlights.title: Carcassonne citadel
highlights.description: Iconic double-walled medieval fortress visible from approach, one of France's most visually striking destinations.

routeStops.name: Boul. de la Cité
routeStops.location: Rue Saint-Louis heart of the Cité

rides.overnightNarrative: Carcassonne is famed for its fortified medieval citadel, La Cité, with double walls and watchtowers that appear almost mythical. Restored in the 19th century by Viollet-le-Duc, it embodies the idealized image of a medieval fortress. Historically, it played a key role during the Albigensian Crusade against the Cathars. Today, Carcassonne attracts visitors drawn to its dramatic skyline, blending legend and history into one of France’s most iconic and visually striking destinations.
rides.dayShape: Castelnaudary → Carcassonne: 22.3 miles, 70 ft elevation. Short flat day. Route passes through Bram and Le Somail on or near Canal du Midi. Carcassonne—dramatic medieval fortress city—marks final fortress landmark before Mediterranean approach.

highlights.title: Minervois wine region
highlights.description: Vineyard-dominated landscape providing distinctive terrain and local wine culture worth exploring and tasting.

highlights.title: Variable towpath conditions
highlights.description: More technical riding sections adding challenge and variety to cycling experience on this day.

highlights.title: Capestang collegiate church
highlights.description: Prominent landmark visible from distance, architectural achievement and spiritual center of small town.

rides.overnightNarrative: Capestang grew around its imposing collegiate church, Saint-Étienne, whose unfinished tower reflects ambitious medieval plans. The town sits along the Canal du Midi, giving it a quiet but steady connection to trade and travel. Nearby wetlands and vineyards shape its landscape. Capestang is known for its relaxed pace and local wine culture, offering a contrast to larger destinations. It remains a place where canal life, agriculture, and history intersect in understated but authentic ways.
rides.dayShape: Carcassonne → Capestang: 57.3 miles, 120 ft elevation. Longest day, but flat. Full day along Canal du Midi—one of the world's most beautiful waterway routes. Route passes through Homps and Paraza. Steady pace through iconic landscape.

highlights.title: Oppidum d'Ensérune
highlights.description: Ancient hilltop archaeological site providing historical context and views over surrounding Languedoc landscape.

highlights.title: Nine Fonseranes Locks
highlights.description: Iconic staircase of locks descending toward sea, engineering marvel and visually stunning canal feature.

highlights.title: Coastal lagoons and Thau Basin
highlights.description: Transition to Mediterranean landscape, unique wetland ecosystem and final geographic marker before arrival.

routeStops.name: Boulangerie de la Place
routeStops.location: Place Jean Jaurès facing the church

routeStops.name: La Tropézienne
routeStops.location: Near the Quai de la Résistance

rides.overnightNarrative: Sète, founded in the 17th century to support the Canal du Midi, is a vibrant port city linking inland waterways to the Mediterranean. Known as the “Venice of Languedoc,” its canals define the urban landscape. It hosts the lively water jousting festival, a centuries-old tradition of competitive boat duels. With strong maritime identity, seafood cuisine, and artistic heritage, Sète marks the culmination of the Atlantic-to-Mediterranean journey, blending working port energy with cultural richness.
rides.dayShape: Capestang → Sète: 42.7 miles, 80 ft elevation. Final day to Mediterranean coast. Flat approach through Languedoc wine country. Sète—arrival at salt lagoons and working port—marks Atlantic-to-Mediterranean journey completion.

rides.overnightNarrative: Bordeaux, capital of Nouvelle-Aquitaine, rose to prominence through wine trade and its strategic Garonne River port. Known as “Port of the Moon,” its crescent harbor fueled global commerce in the 18th century. The city’s limestone architecture reflects immense wealth from that era. Bordeaux also carries complex history tied to Atlantic trade. Today, it blends grand boulevards, vibrant markets, and a deep wine culture, serving as both cultural capital and gateway to southwestern France’s vineyards.
